 {"id":"bd-193","title":"E2E: Security regression suite (capability & sandbox)","description":"# Goal
Build **end-to-end security tests** that prove capability enforcement and sandbox boundaries with detailed logs.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Test cases:
  - path traversal + symlink escape
  - forbidden host/network policy
  - env key denial
  - oversized payload / output limits
  - denied `exec` capability blocks `child_process.spawn` and `process.kill`
  - wasm bridge limits: memory/table limits, denied imports, trap mapping (PiWasm)
- Each failure must emit a structured log entry with capability, scope, and deny reason.
- Negative tests ensure no partial side effects occurred.

# Why (User Value)
- Trustworthy, repeatable proof that PiJS is safer than Node/Bun.

# Tests
- E2E scripts that run with verbose logging, produce a summary report, and archive per-test artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
- [ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead
- [ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
- [ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
- [ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
- [ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes

","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:15:29Z"},{"id":292,"issue_id":"bd-19rf","author":"LavenderRobin","text":"DISCOVERY CHANNELS + REPEATABLE QUERIES (2026-02-05)

Purpose
- Provide a deterministic “source checklist” so future agents can repeat discovery and converge on the same candidate set.

A) Official Pi sources (baseline)
- pi-mono examples/extensions README + dirs (already vendored once; keep as must-pass baseline)
- buildwithpi.ai packages + docs (if package JSON is exposed, treat it as authoritative metadata)
- badlogic gists tagged/mentioned as extensions

B) OpenClaw / marketplace ecosystems (new major surface)
- Identify the canonical OpenClaw repo/org and any associated “marketplace / hub / directory” (ClawHub or equivalent).
- Prefer machine-readable indexes (JSON feeds, GraphQL endpoints, API responses) over scraping HTML.
- Export raw dumps (with timestamps) so the inventory can be regenerated.

C) GitHub repo discovery (keyword-based)
Run repo searches, record top N results + reasons, and archive query strings.
- Query ideas (adjust to reduce noise):
  - "pi agent" extension
  - "buildwithpi" extension
  - "pi-mono" extension
  - "pi" "registerTool" extension
  - topic-based: topic:pi-extension OR topic:pi-agent OR topic:buildwithpi (if topics exist)

D) GitHub code discovery (signature-based)
Goal: find repos that contain *actual extension entrypoints*.
- Search for common registration patterns:
  - "registerTool(" AND ("export default" OR "ExtensionAPI" OR "ctx.")
  - "registerCommand(" OR "/"-command patterns
  - "registerProvider(" (custom providers)
  - "onEvent" / lifecycle hook names (session/tool/cancel)
  - "ui." / rpc UI calls used by extensions
- Heuristic: prefer hits in TypeScript/JavaScript; then validate as “true Pi extension” by checking for Pi protocol usage.

E) npm discovery
- Search npm for packages mentioning Pi Agent / buildwithpi / pi-mono / extension API.
- Capture download counts + dependents (popularity evidence).

F) Cross-reference mining (mentions)
- Search README/docs/issues across discovered repos for:
  - "pi extension" / "pi-agent extension" / "buildwithpi" / "pi-mono" mentions
  - Links to gists or extension bundles
- This tends to find “hidden” but widely used extensions.

Required output
- A list of sources + the exact queries executed (copy/paste ready).
- For each query: the date/time, tool used (GitHub UI/API/gh), and how many candidates it yielded.
- A “noise notes” section: which queries were too broad and what filters improved them.

 {"id":"bd-19rt","title":"Phase 8: CI Integration and Continuous Conformance","description":"# Phase 8: CI Integration and Continuous Conformance

## Purpose
Conformance testing must run on EVERY PR to prevent regressions. A single missed comparison could break third-party extensions.

## CI Pipeline Design

### Fast Path (every PR, < 5min)
- Run Tier 1 (14 simple extensions) in differential mode
- Run Tier 2-3 (18 extensions) registration-only comparison
- Total: ~32 extensions, ~3 minutes

### Full Path (nightly, < 30min)
- All 60 official extensions, full differential
- All community extensions, full differential
- Performance benchmarks
- Total: ~200 extensions, ~20 minutes

### Weekly Path (weekend, < 2hr)
- Everything in Full Path
- 1-hour stress test
- Full npm/third-party corpus
- Conformance report generation and archival

## Implementation
- tests/ext_conformance_runner.rs with #[cfg] feature flags
- cargo test --features conformance-fast (fast path)
- cargo test --features conformance-full (full path)
- cargo test --features conformance-stress (weekly)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fast path runs on every PR and passes
- Full path runs nightly and produces report
- Any conformance regression blocks PR merge
- Conformance report archived per run","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:25:50.751897716Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T00:54:11.387560196Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T00:53:58.955865836Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-19rt","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:25:50.756187304Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":458,"issue_id":"bd-19rt","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Epic complete: CI has 4-tier conformance pipeline (fast/full/full-scenario/weekly). Weekly runs community/npm/third-party corpus. Both child beads (bd-2s1z, bd-7rmt) already closed.","created_at":"2026-02-06T00:54:11Z"}]}

- src/extensions.rs (CompatibilityScanner, ExtensionManager)
- tests/e2e_extension_registration.rs (existing test patterns)

 {"id":"bd-1f5","title":"Extensions: QuickJS runtime + Pi event loop (no Node/Bun)","description":"Background:
- JS compatibility requires deterministic event loop semantics without Node/Bun.
- Assume **QuickJS has no WebAssembly**: wasm-using JS bundles must use the PiWasm bridge (bd-1ry) or Tier A WASM components.

Steps:
- Embed QuickJS via safe Rust bindings and expose a minimal Pi event loop.
- Implement microtask + promise ordering and timer scheduling per bd-123.
- Provide a hostcall bridge: JS promises resolve/reject only via the connector dispatcher (timeouts/cancel/policy errors correctly mapped).
- Load/inject required compatibility shims (Node core modules + globals) via the extc pipeline + module loader contract.
- Emit structured logs for JS task scheduling, hostcalls, policy decisions, and wasm-bridge activity.

Acceptance (hard):
- JS-tier runtime can execute the **full pinned sample set** where applicable: **16/16 extensions in `docs/extension-sample.json` run with no manual source edits**, using shims/rewrites/bridge as needed.
- Deterministic ordering is testable and reproducible under harness control.

Notes:
- Node core module shims are tracked in bd-3d0 (non-child_process) and bd-2sr (child_process).
- WebAssembly-in-JS is tracked in bd-1ry.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

## Background
Phase 1 (bd-1oj9) implements basic provider registration with standard APIs.
This phase adds support for custom streaming via JavaScript callbacks.

## API Addition
```typescript
pi.registerProvider(name, {
  // ... base config
  streamSimple?: (request) => AsyncIterable<string>
});
```

## Implementation Requirements
1. Async callback bridge from Rust to JS
2. Stream chunks from JS handler to agent loop
3. Error handling for JS exceptions during streaming
4. Cancellation support (cancel JS stream on interrupt)

## Technical Challenges
- JS -> Rust async iteration
- Back-pressure handling
- Error propagation across language boundary

## Test Plan
- Unit test: custom streamSimple called for provider
- Integration test: streaming works end-to-end
- Test: cancellation stops JS stream

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions.rs (stream bridge)
- src/extensions_js.rs (async callback support)
- src/provider.rs (use extension stream)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:36:36.961318176Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T05:30:01.456330680Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T05:29:25.285844625Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-39q8","depends_on_id":"bd-1oj9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:36:59.163581435Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":247,"issue_id":"bd-39q8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closed. +# Benchmark JSONL Schema Reference
+
+> Auto-generated. Do not edit manually.
+
+## Registered Schemas
+
+| Schema | Description |
+|---|---|
+| `pi.ext.rust_bench.v1` | Rust QuickJS extension benchmark event (load, tool call, event hook) |
+| `pi.ext.legacy_bench.v1` | Legacy pi-mono (Node.js) extension benchmark event |
+| `pi.perf.workload.v1` | PiJS workload harness output (tool call throughput) |
+| `pi.perf.budget.v1` | Performance budget check result |
+| `pi.perf.budget_summary.v1` | Aggregate budget summary with pass/fail counts |
+| `pi.ext.conformance_report.v2` | Per-extension conformance report event |
+| `pi.ext.conformance_summary.v2` | Aggregate conformance summary with per-tier breakdowns |
+
+## Environment Fingerprint
+
+Every benchmark record SHOULD include an `env` object with:
+
+| Field | Type | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `os` | string | Operating system name and version |
+| `arch` | string | CPU architecture (x86_64, aarch64) |
+| `cpu_model` | string | CPU model string from /proc/cpuinfo or sysinfo |
+| `cpu_cores` | integer | Logical CPU core count |
+| `mem_total_mb` | integer | Total system memory in megabytes |
+| `build_profile` | string | Cargo build profile: debug or release |
+| `git_commit` | string | Short git commit hash of the build |
+| `features` | string[] | Active Cargo feature flags |
+| `config_hash` | string | SHA-256 of env fields for dedup |
+
+## Required Fields by Schema
+
+### `pi.ext.rust_bench.v1`
+
+| Field | Type | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `schema` | string | Always `"pi.ext.rust_bench.v1"` |
+| `runtime` | string | Always `"pi_agent_rust"` |
+| `scenario` | string | Benchmark scenario (e.g., `ext_load_init/load_init_cold`) |
+| `extension` | string | Extension ID being benchmarked |
+| `runs` | integer | Number of runs (load scenarios) |
+| `iterations` | integer | Number of iterations (throughput scenarios) |
+| `summary` | object | `{count, min_ms, p50_ms, p95_ms, p99_ms, max_ms}` |
+| `elapsed_ms` | float | Total elapsed time in milliseconds |
+| `per_call_us` | float | Per-call latency in microseconds |
+| `calls_per_sec` | float | Throughput (calls per second) |
+
+### `pi.ext.legacy_bench.v1`
+
+Same structure as `pi.ext.rust_bench.v1` with:
+- `runtime` = `"legacy_pi_mono"`
+- `node` object: `{version, platform, arch}`
+
+### `pi.perf.workload.v1`
+
+| Field | Type | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `scenario` | number | Workload scenario name |
+| `iterations` | number | Number of outer iterations |
+| `tool_calls_per_iteration` | number | Tool calls per iteration |
+| `total_calls` | number | Total tool calls executed |
+| `elapsed_ms` | number | Total elapsed milliseconds |
+| `per_call_us` | number | Per-call latency in microseconds |
+| `calls_per_sec` | number | Throughput (calls per second) |
+
+## Determinism Requirements
+
+1. **Stable key ordering**: JSON keys are sorted alphabetically within each record
+2. **No floating point in keys**: Use string or integer identifiers
+3. **Timestamps**: ISO 8601 with seconds precision (`2026-02-06T01:00:00Z`)
+4. **Config hash**: SHA-256 of concatenated env fields for dedup
+5. **One record per line**: Standard JSONL (newline-delimited JSON)
